speaker: interviewer
text: So March 3, 1947 you arrived in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: March 7, '47 I arrived in New York. And the-- and the Joint what helped us, Joint and HIAS, They took us to a hotel. They gave us a room. They gave us food. They treated us fantastic, fantastic. But my husband's a young man. He didn't wanted no charities. He wanted to go to work, you know. So they gave us an apartment, they bought for us an apartment downtown on sixth floor walk up. But I was still young and healthy. So I-- healthy I was not too much. I mean, physically I was all right, but I was nervous. I was very much-- I was-- but that's OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we got the apartment there. We moved in. It was very nice. It was a Jewish neighborhood. And my husband was running day after day looking for work.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not easy not knowing the language perfect. We could say hello, how are you, but we weren't, you know, fluently in English. It's not so easy. And I couldn't get a job, because I didn't go to get a job, because I didn't feel so very well, you know, to go to look for a job. It was a too big city for me. Too big, too much.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the beginning it was very hard, very hard. But we knew where we are, and that made us happy. So my husband grabbed here a job, there a job.

speaker: interviewee
text: He told you, whatever you know, he did it. He was taxi driver. He was everything. But I always got food to eat. Everything was always for me. He brought me everything. He took care of me. And then later on I got pregnant. And I-- I didn't believe it that I am pregnant. I didn't believe it, because after Auschwitz you never know if you can get pregnant, you know. But then I went to a big doctor on Fifth Avenue. My husband at that time was working in a factory, garment factory. And the owner of the factory saw his face, that he's very sad, very sad.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said to him, why are you so sad? He said, I don't know. My wife doesn't feel good. She thinks that she is pregnant.

speaker: interviewee
text: She went to a doctor downtown. It was some doctor. Don't ask.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the doctor told her that she's not, that she has a tumor. She's going to need a operation. So I am heartbroken. What to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, look. Here is a piece of paper with the address. Take this and go there and there. Take your wife to him. Let him take care of her.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to-- this was Park Avenue. It was one of the big gynecologist doctors. He took me there to him, and he-- after he-- he had took care of me, he said to my husband, look, you better smile, because you're going to have a gorgeous baby. You're going to have a baby. She's pregnant. So we got happy. And that was the happiest day in my life. I was dreaming to have a baby, even though thinking always about the one whom I lost. And I had a boy, God bless him, a big boy. He's big. What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Elliot. Elliot. He's after my husband's father, Eliyahu. And then God gave me a grandson, also a gorgeous boy, graduate college this year, Michigan University. Now he's working for that crazy guy, for that Stern.

speaker: interviewer
text: Howard Stern.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work when you came here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, I was working 30 years in United States in New York. I got the job. First I got the job after my boy was bigger. I could leave him in the nursery, so I went for a job to American Express office, Wall Street I think they are. And there's maybe 20 girls looking there for jobs. Somehow they took me. You know, I don't know if they were pity or something. They took me and I worked there a long time. Then I took a job in a life insurance office at Columbus Circle. There I was working long years. I worked myself up. I start as a filing girl, you know. And I was-- every few months, they gave me a better job, a better job, and I love it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then I was the secretary to 10 lawyers, because they had a lot of lawyers, and they had to have-- what was our job? Our job was to preparing papers for them every day to court. You had to find all the papers, and the dates, and then everything had to-- I was very good at that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I worked a long time there, till my husband start to build in New Jersey. He got-- he told you. He got the job. He start to build, so he stopped me from working. I retired in young age, let's say.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel about being in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can tell you only one thing, fantastic, fantastic. I love every minute of it. I never felt so free, so-- so good. I can tell you, as I told you about the Pope in Rome, whenever I come back from Europe I feel like bending down and kissing the stones here that I am back. I love it. I never had a country which I would be a-- a-- a-- a citizen, never. Because my family didn't have citizen papers in Poland, because they had papers from the Russian-- Russian-- even not Russian, because they run away from Russia. So we couldn't get citizenship.

speaker: interviewer
text: In light of the life you've lived and the experiences that you've had, is there a message you would like to leave for future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd like to leave a message. The young people should realize that they are in America. They are free. They can do with their life whatever they wish, but I hope they do it the right way. They don't go the wrong way. And I tell you what I was talking in Miami in the university. I said be good, learn, love your parents, love your family, and appreciate United States. We're the best country in the whole world. The best.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Thank you for being here. Mrs. Wertheim, would you please introduce your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Wertheim?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, me. On my left, first in the blue is my husband Sam. Next to him is my son Elliot. In the back of me is my daughter-in-law, Ellen Wertheim. Next to her is my grandson, Aaron.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would any of you like to say something? Mr. Wertheim?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as they were introduced already, I can only tell you that that was my accomplishment here in this country. I raised a-- a beautiful family, and that I'm very proud of them. And especially of recently which graduate my grandson from University of Michigan. And I think this will be something for the-- for them to-- to remember us by.

speaker: interviewer
text: Elliot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I guess I can say that I'm happy to be here on a lot of levels. I'm proud to be their son. And I think that I'm, I guess, living proof of the will to survive, and the Jewish heritage, and the absoluteness of the human spirit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ellen?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd just like to say that I'm honored to be a part of this family, and that Edith and Sam have always treated me like I was their own daughter. And I feel like I'm their daughter, and I'm proud to be here and to be a part of this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Aaron?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm also very happy to be here. And I suppose I can't imagine two people that I respect more than both of them. As I am sort of distanced from the whole experience as being third generation, I can only see it through their eyes. And the way that they've shown it to me and the way that they've gone through it is just astounding to me and astounding to everyone I know. And I love the fact that we're all able to be here together, and that they've made it through.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you to everyone.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my brother, the oldest brother in the family. The name was Herman Pewzner. He was a conductor in the symphony orchestra in Krakow. He was very famous. He was like a father to me after my father died.

speaker: interviewer
text: I miss him very much. He was wonderful. That's what I can say. What can I say more? He was good. He was very good. So on the left is my sister's daughter, older daughter. She was 16 years old. Name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Sabina. Next to her is my sister in the black dress. Her name was Helena Sauber. She died in the war. Next to her is my brother-in-law, Abraham Sauber. Next to him is the little daughter, Marylia. She died together with her mother in the war. And this-- this my daughter and my niece on the left side in the white dress, she came to United States after the war. She got married. She got two children. One of them is a plastic surgeon, well-known, on Park Avenue, Mark Erlich. And she got a daughter, Helen, after her mother named. Her daughter and her son are living in New York. But my niece, she died a few years ago after a long sickness. So I have nothing left from that side. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is my brother, Henry Pewzner. He was living in Warsaw, Poland. He was a pianist. He was playing on the radio. He was a famous pianist in Warsaw. And he-- he went from Warsaw. He ran away to Russia. And there, or they killed him or he died, but I didn't know nothing about him.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken just before the war a few years, like three, four years before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get all of these photos that were taken before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember, but I think somebody gave me-- somebody gave me the pictures who got them here in the United States. Because I couldn't get one picture out from-- from Poland, not one picture. I don't have pictures from my mother. I don't have from my father. I don't have pictures. This I treasure. This is what I have, a few pictures. This is a picture of my brother, who was only two years older than me. I loved him very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was always taking care of me and joking around with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Zygmund, Zygmund Pewzner. He was working as a bank cashier. He was a very brilliant, brilliant boy. He ran away to Russia in the war, and he got sick in Russia and he died there. Didn't come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That photo was taken a few years before the war. Now this is my savior. He saved my life--

speaker: interviewer
text: Name?

speaker: interviewee
text: --through the war. His name Oskar Schindler, our hero. He saved 1,300 people. He came to Auschwitz to pay diamonds and money to take us out, 300 girls whom they took by mistake to Auschwitz. We were supposed to go to Brinnlitz, but he gave away everything what he got to pay for us, to take us to Brinnlitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: When and where was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was before-- that was after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- I think that was in Germany after the war, after my wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: I married in '46, this must be '47 or '48. '47 when I left for United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: What city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Regensburg, Bavaria. This is a picture taken in Regensburg, Bavaria two days before my wedding. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: 1946-- yes, February 1946. This is my cousin, Leo Liebgold, who came from United States to Regensburg to Bavaria to look for family, and he found me there. He prepared my wedding. But then he was in the army. He was in CIA, I think, and he had to leave for United States. We were still there. But when we came to United States, we got very close. That was only that cousin who's left. He was left, but he died two years ago. He died after a long sickness. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This is a picture already in the United States with my baby. He was one year old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Name, Elliot Wertheim. That picture was taken in Washington, DC where we were invited to a luncheon to Yitzhak Rabin. And so he wanted to get a picture with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in 1973, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you name everyone in the photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left is a friend of ours, Mrs. [? Bullstein. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: In the middle?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the middle is Yitzhak Rabin. And on the right is me. This picture was taken on the-- on the honoring party for Steven Spielberg in Waldorf Astoria 1993, I think, yeah. We were greeting him. And I was so happy to be that time with him, because he's a wonderful, wonderful man, brilliant. And we like him very much. He's wonderful.
